coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of citric acid-based carbon dots by imidazole toward novel green corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dongping
Ye, Yuwei
Su, Yue
Liu, Shuang
Gong, Dirong
Zhao, Haichao
coredata.dc:description: In order to address the environment pollution problem in the cleaning process of metal, a green corrosion inhibitor with the size of 3–5.5 nm was synthesized from imidazole ionic liquids and citric acid-based carbon dots. The inhibition behaviors of this inhibitor for Q235 steel in 1 M HCl and 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were studied by electrochemical measurement, surface analysis and weight loss. The dependence of corrosion current density and impedance modulus of Q235 steel on the different concentrations of corrosion inhibitor solutions showed that the as-prepared inhibitor presented a valid protective effect on steel in both corrosive solutions. In the meantime, the inhibition efficiency of steel in 1 M HCl solution was higher than that in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The mechanism of corrosion inhibitor was discussed with isothermal adsorption model. The result revealed that the Langmuir adsorption was followed and the mechanism was physicochemical adsorption in these two solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green chemistry
Corrosion inhibitor
Cl− contained solution
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of imidazole ionic liquid modified carbon dots (IM-CDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization spectra of CDs and IM-CDs (a) FTIR; (b) UV–vis; (c) Raman; (d) XPS- Full spectra; (e) XPS-C1s; (f) XPS-N1s.
(a) TEM image of IM-CDs; (b) Size histogram of IM-CDs; (c) SPM image of IM-CDs; (d) Height distribution of IM-CDs.
OCP values and Tafel curves of Q235 steel in various test solutions (a)(b) HCl solution; (c)(d) NaCl solution.
EIS images of Q235 steel in various test environments after 24 h immersion
Equivalent circuit model of corrosion process

Current density profiles of Q235 electrode in various test solutions and immersion time (a) HCl-0 mg/L-24 h; (b) HCl-25 mg/L-24 h; (c) HCl-200 mg/L-24 h; (d) NaCl-0 mg/L-24 h; (e) NaCl-25 mg/L-24 h; (f) NaCl-200 mg/L-24 h.
Corrosion morphology and element distribution of Q235 carbon steel in various test solutions and immersion time.
3D morphology of Q235 steel in various test solutions and immersion time.
XPS element content and Raman spectra of Q235 steel surface after 24 h immersion in various test solutions (a)(c) 1 M HCl; (b)(d) 3.5 wt% NaCl.
Langmuir adsorption isotherms of Q235 steel surface in various test solutions (a) 1 M HCl; (b) 3.5 wt% NaCl.
Corrosion rate of Q235 steel in various test solutions and immersion time
Corrosion parameters of Q235 steel in various test solutions.
Impedance parameters of Q235 carbon steel in various test solutions.

head.ce:title: Functionalization of citric acid-based carbon dots by imidazole toward novel green corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo, 315201, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies
Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo, 315201, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to address the environment pollution problem in the cleaning process of metal, a green corrosion inhibitor with the size of 3–5.5 nm was synthesized from imidazole ionic liquids and citric acid-based carbon dots. The inhibition behaviors of this inhibitor for Q235 steel in 1 M HCl and 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were studied by electrochemical measurement, surface analysis and weight loss. The dependence of corrosion current density and impedance modulus of Q235 steel on the different concentrations of corrosion inhibitor solutions showed that the as-prepared inhibitor presented a valid protective effect on steel in both corrosive solutions. In the meantime, the inhibition efficiency of steel in 1 M HCl solution was higher than that in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The mechanism of corrosion inhibitor was discussed with isothermal adsorption model. The result revealed that the Langmuir adsorption was followed and the mechanism was physicochemical adsorption in these two solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion has been a global problem in the rapid development of industrial technology, which involves many fields of national production (Zhang et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2009). The global economic losses caused by corrosion are up to 3% of GDP every year (Qian et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2019a), and about one-third of steel is lost due to the corrosion (Pikaar et al., 2014; Ruiz-Cabañas et al., 2016; Li et al., 2015). Therefore, various metal corrosion protection methods have been developed in the industry (Chao et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2012). Among them, the corrosion inhibitor has been widely used in energy, transportation, construction and industries due to its diverse advantages such as low cost, simple process, strong adaptability and economic efficiency (Finšgar et al., 2008; Antonijević et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2017).

In recent years, a series of corrosion inhibitors with high corrosion protective efficiency have been successfully developed (Qiang et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2016; AI-Amiery et al., 2014). However, most of the traditional corrosion inhibitors including chromate, mercury salts, phosphorus-containing compounds are toxic, which have been restricted (Kowsari et al., 2016; Aslam et al., 2018; Casaletto et al., 2018). With the deepening of sustainable development thinking and the growth of people's awareness of environmental protection, the development and application of green corrosion inhibitors have been the focus of research (Alibakhshi et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with size below 10 nm (Xu et al., 2014; Kai et al., 2015). Thanks to the low toxicity (Genc et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2017), strong chemical inertness (Wang et al., 2017; Vã et al., 2017), environmental friendliness (Cao et al., 2007; Ya-Ping et al., 2006), convenient preparation (Zu et al., 2017) and easy functionalization (Liu et al., 2010), CDs have received increasing academic and industrial attention and been used in the field of anti-corrosion (Cui et al., 2017, 2018; Olivares-Xometl et al., 2014). Recently, nitrogen-doped carbon dots have been reported as novel green inhibitors for carbon steel. Cui et al. (2018) acquired a new type of carbon dots by hydrothermal carbonization of aminosalicylic acid as raw material and the corrosion of metal was greatly inhibited after the addition of the carbon dots in corrosion solution. The same group also reported the preparation of N-doped CDs by hydrothermal decomposition of p-phenylenediamine (p-CDs) and o-phenylenediamine (o-CDs), and the resulted carbon dots had high inhibition efficiency in the protection of steel in hydrochloric acid solution (Cui et al., 2017).

As a non-metallic corrosion inhibitor, imidazole ionic liquid has been studied benefited from its excellent chemical stability (Olivares-Xometl et al., 2014; Likhanova et al., 2010), non-toxic nature (Zhang and Hua, 2009; Xiao et al., 2009) and negligible volatility (Xin et al., 2011). For instance, Kowsari et al. (2014) found that the corrosion current density was reduced and the corrosion inhibition ability was enhanced with the increase of imidazole ionic liquids with a gemini cationic molecular structure (TSIL) concentration in 1 mol/L HCl on steel. Lozano et al. (2014) found 1,3-dibencilimidazolio acetate (DBImA) and 1,3-dibencilimidazolio dodecanoate (DBImL) are both the mix-type inhibitors. In order to further improve the performance, the introduction of one or more functional groups to form a functionalized imidazolium ionic liquid has been a common modification technique (Luo et al., 2014).

In this study, we designed a novel green inhibitor consisting of carbon dots and imidazole, where citric acid and imidazole ionic liquids were used as raw materials. It was found that the functionalization of citric acid-based carbon dots (IM-CDs) have excellent water solubility. Therefore, the inhibition efficiency of the prepared IM-CDs as a corrosion inhibitor was tested. In addition, the inhibition mechanism was elucidated by the Langmuir adsorption isotherms.

Anhydrous citric acid was provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation. 1-Methylimidazole, (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), ethanol, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 39%), nitric acid (HNO3, 69%), 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide and N-carboxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Carbon steel Q235 was provided by the Sheng Xin Technology Co., Ltd. with the elementary composition (wt%): P 0.016, Si 0.07, Ni 0.01, Mn 0.28, C 0.18, S 0.006, Cr 0.02, Cu 0.05, Fe balance.

The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by the reported method (Li et al., 2014). In detail, anhydrous citric acid (2 g) was placed at a 100 mL flask and heated at 200 °C for 30 min, during which a pale yellow liquid formed at 180 °C and turned into an orange-red at 200 °C. Then, 100 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution was added with continuous stirring for 15 min and the solution was neutralized with aqueous HNO3 solution (1 mol/L). Finally, the resultant solution was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (number average molecular weight of 1000 g/mol) in deionized water for 12 h. During this process, the water was renewed every 4 h, and the obtained product was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 20 h.

The imidazole ionic liquid was prepared by the similar method (Obata, 2006). In detail, 5 g of 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide was weighed and dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol, and continuously stirred for 0.5 h in a 100 mL of volumetric flask. Thereafter, 2 g of 1-methylimidazole was slowly added into the volumetric flask. The reaction was kept for 10 h at 90 °C and the 1-methyl-3-(2′-aminoethane)imidazolium bromide was resulted by evaporation of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of imidazole ionic liquid modified carbon dots (IM-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 showed the synthetic scheme for preparation of IM-CDs. In the synthetic process, as-prepared CDs (2 mg) and (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (1 g) were mixed and dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water to form a CDs solution. Imidazole ionic liquid (1 g) was dissolved into 20 mL of ethanol, where 0.5 mL of triethylamine was slowly added to obtain an imidazole solution. Then, the CDs solution was added into the imidazole solution and the obtained solution was stirred for 12 h. After dialysis treatment using dialysis membrane with a number average molecular weight of 1000 g/mol, the product was rinsed with deionized water and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 20 h.

Steel Q235 was fabricated into electrode for electrochemical experiments in corrosive media. The exposed face of electrode was sequentially polished with 400, 800, 1500 and 2000 mesh sandpapers. After that, the electrode was washed three times with propanol and ethanol, wiped with a non-woven fabric, and dried prior to use. For corrosion media, two corrosive solutions (1 M HCl solution and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution) were prepared for the testing environment. The solution systems differing in concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 mg/L) of corrosion inhibitor were prepared for electrochemical testing.

As prepared IM-CDs was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible adsorption spectroscopy (UV–vis), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning probe microscope (SPM). In detail, the structure of IM-CDs was analyzed by NICOLET 6700 FTIR spectrometer, Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer and Raman spectra (Renishaw inVia Reflex), respectively. XPS analysis (AXIS ULTRA DLD) was applied to test the bonding status and chemical composition of as-prepared inhibitor. Morphology and grain size of inhibitor were investigated by TF20 TEM and Dimension 3100 SPM.

Electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used for the measurements of open circuit potential (OCP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to evaluate the corrosion performance of as-prepared inhibitor. A traditional three-electrode system was employed for electrochemical testing. The working electrode, reference electrode and auxiliary electrode were coated Q235 carbon steel electrode, saturated calomel electrode and the platinum plate, respectively. OCP measurement was tested for 30 min to obtain a steady state of system. Then, the EIS curve of steel in different immersion time was tested at an amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range of 100 kHz–10 mHz. Three parallel experiments in the electrochemical test were conducted to ensure the experiment repeatability. The obtained EIS data was disposed through ZSimDemo 3.30d. The local corrosion situation of steel was acquired through scanning vibrating electrode technology (SVET). The vibration amplitude was 30 μm, the vibration frequency was 80 Hz and the test area was 2000 × 2000 μm2.

The surface morphology of electrode was observed by S4800 SEM after the electrode was etched in the etchant solution for different time. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was selected to analyze the corrosion product on the exposed side of electrode. 3D LSM700 laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) was used to examine the surface roughness of as-prepared electrode.

The Q235 steel with size of 1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm was used for the weight loss test. Five specimens were weighted three times before test. All specimens were immersed in corrosion solution at 25 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the specimens were extracted and placed into the descaling solution to eliminate corrosion products. Rinsed with distilled water and ethanol, the specimens were dried by cool air and re-weighted accurately to calculate their average corrosion rates. Corrosion rate (V
corr ) could be defined by the following equation (1) (Zhang and Hua, 2009):
(1)
V
c o r r
=
w
1
−
w
0
S × t
where w
0 and w
1 were the weights of Q235 carbon steel in corrosive medium without and with inhibitors, respectively. S was the total specimen area (cm2) exposure to medium and t was the time of Q235 carbon steel in corrosive medium (h).

The chemical structures of CDs and IM-CDs were characterized by FTIR. The adsorption peaks at 1184.6, 1400, 1642.8, 2958.1 and 3412 cm−1 could be ascribed to C-OH, COO-, CO, C-H and O-H in citric acid-based CDs, respectively (Fig. 2 a) (Genc et al., 2017). However, the formation of CC bond (1630 cm−1) indicated that citric acid was converted to CDs (Xu et al., 2014). In addition, new peaks were also observed in IM-CDs. For example, the broad adsorption peaks at 3364 cm−1 could be assigned to O-H and N-H (Cui et al., 2018). And the adsorption peaks at 1499.9, 1549.9 and 1639.8 cm−1 were ascribed to N-H, C-N, N-CO, respectively (Olivares-Xometl et al., 2014). The generation of these functionalities implied that the reaction between the amino of imidazole and the carboxyl of CDs occurred.

The UV–visible spectra of CDs and IM-CDs were depicted in Fig. 2b. The weak adsorption at 342 nm associating to the π–π* electronic transition of sp2 domains was observed in CDs and IM-CDs (Cui et al., 2018). The Raman spectra of CDs and IM-CDs were also shown in Fig. 2c. Two peaks at 1357 and 1580 cm−1 were observed, which corresponded to D and G bands, respectively (Kai et al., 2015), indicating the stretching vibration from sp3 and sp2 hybridization. The XPS spectrum was indicator for the chemical composition and bonding status of IM-CDs. The XPS-Full spectra of CDs and IM-CDs were depicted in Fig. 2d. It could be seen that C and O were the main two elements of CDs. Expectedly, N element was found in IM-CDs after the functionalization. In order to know the existence form of C and N elements, the fitting result was seen in Fig. 2e and f. Four peaks at binding energies of 284.5, 286.2, 287.2, 288.3 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-N, C-O, N-CO bonds, respectively were observed in C1s spectrum (Lu et al., 2017). And, Three peaks at 398.7, 399.8, 401.6 eV ascribed to N-H, N-C and NC, respectively appeared in the N1s spectrum (Cao et al., 2007). These results revealed that the IM was successfully bonded with CDs and functionalized IM-CDs was therefore resulted.

The morphology and dispersion of IM-CDs inhibitor were analyzed by TEM and SPM. As revealed in Fig. 3 a, the IM-CDs inhibitor was a well-dispersed spherical nanoparticle with the average size of 3–5.5 nm by the measurement of particle size analyzer (Fig. 3b). As seen in Fig. 3c, the SPM morphology of IM-CDs inhibitor also displayed a good dispersion. Furthermore, the height of IM-CDs inhibitor was about 3–5 nm by SPM, which was well consistent with the TEM results (Fig. 3d).

Fig. 4 revealed the OCP of Q235 electrode in 1 mol/L HCl and 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions. In HCl environment, the OCP value of steel in IM-CDs solution showed a lower value than that in pure HCl solution, which was well in agreement with previous study (Cui et al., 2018). And the value increased as the inhibitor concentration increased, implying that the corrosion tendency of steel was greatly suppressed (AI-Amiery et al., 2014). The reason could be that the corrosion inhibitor hindered the precipitation and discharge of H+ in the cathode region of metal surface, increasing the overpotential of the hydrogen evolution reaction process, thus slowing down the cathodic process of acid corrosion and restrained the occurrence of corrosion (Liu et al., 2017). In 3.5 wt% NaCl condition, OCP value changed at opposite trend to HCl solution. In the case of Tafel curve, the curve manifested a certain regularity with the transformation of inhibitor concentration. The Tafel curve shifted notably to negative direction by the addition of IM-CDs inhibitor in 1 M HCl environment. Nevertheless, the Tafel curve clearly moved to the positive direction by adding IM-CDs in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, which was similar with the result of polyaspartic acid (Yang et al., 2015).

By calculating the Tafel curve, some parameters were listed in Table 1 . Polarization parameters included the values of corrosion potential (E
corr ), anodic Tafel slope (β
a ), cathodic Tafel slope (β
c ) and corrosion current density (i
corr ), degree of surface coverage (θ) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (η). The degree of surface coverage (θ) and inhibition efficiency (η) were calculated according to the following equations (2) and (3) (Kowsari et al., 2016; Alibakhshi et al., 2019):
(2)
θ =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
(3)
η =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
× 100 %
where i
0
corr and i
corr denoted the corrosion current densities without and with IM-CDs solution, respectively. Obviously, the E
corr value exhibited a similar change law to OCP value as the concentration of corrosion inhibitor increased. However, it was found that the i
corr value of steel in IM-CDs solution was significantly decreased compared with the blank solution, and the value of E
corr was significantly changed, which implied that both the cathode and anode reactions were positively inhibited by the inhibitor (Hu et al., 2016). In other words, the IM-CDs inhibitor may reduce the dissolution of steel and restrain the cathodic reaction. Meanwhile, it was seen from the i
corr value that the inhibition efficiency gradually increased as the concentration of IM-CDs increased, this phenomenon was consistent with the results of other researchers (Ramji et al., 2008; Abdulhameed, 2014). Furthermore, both θ and η values presented a contrary variation trend to i
corr value. It showed that η reached a maximum in 200 mg/L inhibitor solution, which was 92.60% in HCl solution and 83.45% in NaCl solution. These consequences indicated that the as-obtained IM-CDs acted as a mixed corrosion inhibitor and owned excellent protection against steel in corrosive medium. Compared with previous studies, the η of IM-CDs was higher than that of zinc sulfate (Gao et al., 2015), 2-hydroxyphosphonoacetic acid (HPAA) (Gao et al., 2015), polyacrylic acid (PAA) (Umoren et al., 2010), p-CDs (Cui et al., 2018), TSIL (Kowsari et al., 2014), DBImA (Lozano et al., 2014), DBImL (Lozano et al., 2014), polyaspartic acid (PASP) (Bei et al., 2013; Zeino et al. 2018; Cui et al., 2015) and its derivative (Gao et al., 2015) under similar conditions.

The Nyquist and Bode data were shown in Fig. 5 . In the inhibitor-free HCl environment, the diameter of capacitive reactance arc and the impedance modulus value were low, which could be associated with direct exposure of steel surface in corrosive medium. However, the both values were enhanced after the addition of inhibitor in HCl environment, and the enhancement degree was increased as the concentration increased (Fig. 5a and b). In detail, the |Z|
0.01 Hz of steel in pure HCl solution was 12.1 Ω cm2 after 24 h immersion. When the IM-CDs concentration was up to 200 mg/L, the |Z|
0.01 Hz value was enhanced by two orders of magnitude (1115.1 Ω cm2). Accordingly, the increase of anti-corrosion ability could be ascribed to the hetero-atom of IM-CDs, which contained the lone-pair of electrons (Ye et al., 2019b). These electrons could form the coordination with iron atom vacancies on the surface of steel (Cen et al., 2019). Simultaneously, the angular frequency corresponding to the maximum phase angle moved to the low frequency, implying the corrosion of steel was alleviated (Fig. 5c).

In 3.5 wt% NaCl environment, the Nyquist and Bode data presented the analogical pattern (Fig. 5d and e). Nevertheless, the enhancement degree of steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl environment was lower than that in 1 M HCl environment. For example, the |Z|
0.01 Hz of steel in pure NaCl solution was 105.2 Ω cm2. When the concentration of inhibitor was enhanced to 200 mg/L, the |Z|
0.01 Hz value of steel was only enhanced by 432.4 Ω cm2 (Fig. 5e). This result suggested that the high-concentration inhibitor molecules could continuously be adsorbed on the surface of Q235 electrode to form the adsorption film (Ye et al., 2019c). The increased in impedance indicated that the film would become thicker during the adsorption process, eventually leading to strong corrosion protection ability (Kowsari et al., 2016). It was worth mentioning that the corrosion inhibition effect of IM-CDs was obviously stronger than that of IM and CDs in both HCl and NaCl solutions (Fig. S1).

The influence of time on inhibition efficiency of IM-CDs inhibitor was studied and represented in Fig. S2. Compared to those of less immersion time at various test solutions, the impedance modulus values of steel with longer immersion time were greatly enhanced, confirming that the inhibition ability of IM-CDs exhibited a positive relationship with time (Gece, 2008). Moreover, it was also observed from the curve that the radius of capacitor semicircle increased with the immersion time. The phenomenon may be due to an increase in interfacial impedance caused by the adsorption of IM-CDs, and the film formed by the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the steel surface became denser over time, playing a good protective function.

According to the EIS result of all samples, the appropriate equivalent circuit diagram model was generalized in Fig. 6 , and the corresponding electrochemical parameters were extracted and listed in Table 2 . The corrosion process of steel in HCl solution without and with inhibitor corresponded to the model 1 and 2, respectively. However, the model 2 suited for all systems in NaCl solution. In these models, R
s , R
f , R
ct , C
f and C
dl corresponded to the solution resistance, the film resistance, the charge transfer resistance, the film capacitance and the double layer capacitance, respectively. Also, R
ct was chosen to calculate the corrosion inhibition efficiency of γ (Sığırcık et al., 2016):
(4)
γ =
R
c t
−
R
c t , 0
R
c t
× 100 %
where R
ct and R
ct,0 denoted the charge transfer resistances with and without inhibitor solution, respectively. In Table 2, the R
s of steel in HCl or NaCl solutions with all systems were similar. The R
f increased and CEP
f decreased with the increase of inhibitor content, suggesting the formation of adsorption film. Meanwhile, the R
ct presented an ascend trend and CEP
dl showed a downtrend with the increase of inhibitor content. This was due to that the inhibitor molecules with a small dielectric constant could supersede the water molecules with a large dielectric constant, resulting in the reduction of exposure area of steel in corrosion solution. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of γ displayed a similar result to the analysis of Tafel curve.

SVET system can be used for the measurement of local corrosion situation of samples in corrosive solution. The local corrosion result of steel was shown in Fig. 7 . The positive current density of steel was the highest in 1 M HCl environment, indicating that the anode was severely corroded. After the addition of inhibitor, a significant reduction in the anode current density was observed, manifesting that the IM-CDs could be adsorbed on the surface of steel and then inhibit the occurrence of corrosion (Cui et al., 2017). And the inhibition effect was more powerful as the concentration increased. In the meantime, similar trend was observed in NaCl solution. For instance, the max anode current density of steel in pure NaCl solution was about 0.382 μA/cm2. When the inhibitor concentrations increased to 25 and 200 mg/L, the max anode current densities of steel were reduced to 0.015 and 0.004 μA/cm2, respectively.

The steel samples were immersed in HCl and NaCl solution with various concentrations of IM-CDs inhibitor at room temperature for 24 h and 48 h. The surface morphology obtained by SEM was shown in Fig. 8 . The surface of steel was gravely destroyed, and a lot of corrosion products and pits appeared on the surface after immersion for 24 h in HCl solution without corrosion inhibitor. With a few as-prepared inhibitors, the corrosion of steel was obviously improved. It was manifested that the IM-CDs inhibitor emerged a certain defense effect on electrode in acidic medium. As the IM-CDs concentration increased to 200 mg/L, the steel surface was smooth and well protected, suggesting that the protective effect of adsorption film on steel increased with the increase of IM-CDs concentration. This may related to the increased area of covered surface under the high concentration of inhibitor. Therefore, the surface coverage of steel was improved and a good protection result was achieved. Besides, by comparing the results of 24 h and 48 h immersion, the surface of steel remained smooth with the extension of time, manifesting that the adsorption film formed on the metal surface displayed a good stability. During the long-time immersion process, the inhibitor could even be continuously adsorbed on the metal surface, so that the inhibition acted for a long time. Comparatively, the surface of steel was acutely eroded after immersion 24 h in pure NaCl solution. With the inhibitor, the corrosion situation of steel surface was narrowed. As the inhibitor concentration and immersion time increased, the corrosion inhibition effect was much more significant. Through EDS detection, there were some elements (Fe, N) on the surface of corroded steel. Thereinto, iron element was from the electrode surface, nitrogen element was derived from as-prepared IM-CDs inhibitor, further affirming the existence of adsorption film on steel surface.

The 3D morphology analysis was performed for more detain information on the steel surface in Fig. 9 . The surface roughness of steel varied greatly in the solution with and without as-prepared inhibitor. As the corrosion inhibitor concentration and immersion time increased, the surface roughness became smaller and the corrosion inhibition efficiency presented an ascend trend. For instance, with the inhibitor concentration increased from 0 to 200 mg/L, the surface roughness of steel decreased from 1.842 μm to 0.185 μm in HCl solution. At the same time, a slightly decrease was observed after the immersion time increased to 48 h. Similar phenomenon was found in NaCl solution. It was further confirmed that the corrosion inhibitor molecules played a certain inhibition effect.

The surface elements of steel after corrosion were analyzed by EDS and XPS, which were shown in Figs. S3 and 10. For EDS analysis, it could be seen that the proportion of oxygen on the metal surface substantially increased after 24 h immersion in pure HCl solution, indicating that the serious corrosion reaction occurred. Similarly, some base elements of steel (such as Mn, Si, P) were also exposed to the surface, also proving that the surface was severely attacked (Fig. S3). Meanwhile, the oxygen content was decreased while the N element content was increased in the presence of the inhibitor. It may be due to that the IM-CDs inhibitor molecules adsorbed on steel surface, increased the content of N element. Moreover, the IM-CDs impeded the oxidative reaction of steel, resulting in a decreased oxygen content. As the inhibitor concentration increased, Mn, Si and P elements became smaller or even less, indicating that the steel surface was well hidden by the IM-CDs molecules and achieved a certain protective effect. Similar result was detected in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. From XPS analysis, the change rule of oxygen content on the steel surface after 24 h immersion was similar to the EDS analysis as the inhibitor concentration increased (Fig. 10 a and b). In addition, the chemical component of corrosion product was evaluated by Raman analysis. It can be seen that the corrosion products mainly contained α-FeOOH, γ-FeOOH, α-Fe2O3, and Fe3O4 after immersion in both solutions (Fig. 10c and d) (Ye et al., 2019b). As the inhibitor concentration increased, the peaks of corrosion products decreased as a whole, which indicated that the corrosion of steel was suppressed.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the corrosion inhibition mechanism was studied by the adsorption isotherm. According to the relationship between C
inh
/θ and C
inh , the Langmuir adsorption could be concluded. The interaction between the IM-CDs molecule and the steel surface was analyzed by the adsorption free energy (ΔG
ads ) which was obtained through equation (5) (Hamdani et al., 2015):
(5)
Δ
G
ads
0
= − R T I n
( 1000
K
a d s
)
where T was the absolute temperature (298 K), R was the molar gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1). In addition, the adsorption equilibrium constant (K
ads ) could be calculated from the linear formula of isothermal adsorption (Xu et al., 2014):
(6)
C
i n h
θ
=
1
K
a d s
+
C
i n h

Among them,θ and C were the surface coverage and inhibitor concentration, respectively. In general, the higher the Kads was, the stronger the adsorption behavior was (Nabel et al., 2012). The Kads value of steel in HCl solution with inhibitor was calculated to be 0.13314 L/mg, one order of magnitude higher than that in NaCl solution, implying a stronger adsorption involved. Furthermore, the physical absorption and chemisorption were common types between corrosion inhibitor molecule and carbon steel surface, and were closely related to ΔGads value (Lagrenée et al., 2002). Specifically, the value of physical adsorption was about -20 kJ·mol−1 or lower, and the ΔGads value of chemisorption was more negative than -40 kJ·mol−1 (Olasunkanmi et al., 2015). As can be seen, the ΔGads values were about -29.23 and -25.45 kJ·mol−1 in HCl and NaCl environments, respectively, indicating that the adsorption mechanism of IM-CDs may be a combination of physical absorption and chemical adsorption. Thus, the corrosion inhibition process was spontaneous and beneficial to the generation of a protective film.

As shown in Fig. 12 , the corrosion rate was significantly reduced after adding the inhibitor in both environments. In addition, as the concentration of IM-CDs increased, the corrosion rates of steel in both environments were decreased to some degree. When the inhibitor concentration was 200 mg/L, the corrosion rate of steel in HCl and NaCl solutions decreased to 3.15 × 10−4 g⋅cm−2⋅h−1 and 4.60 × 10−4 g⋅cm−2⋅h−1, respectively, which was one order of magnitude less than that of steel in pure solution and similar to previous studies (Cui et al., 2017, 2018). This trend was consistent with the previously corrosion inhibition efficiency. Analogously, the connection between immersion time and the corrosion rate of steel was explored. The corrosion rate of steel in pure solution increased with the immersion time in the absence of corrosion inhibitor protection. Comparatively, with IM-CDs, the corrosion rate of steel was significantly suppressed after immersion.

Novel citric acid-based carbon dots containing imidazole-based ionic liquid were synthesized and characterized by FTIR, UV–vis, Raman and XPS. The particle size of as-prepared inhibitor was about 3–5.5 nm as observed by TEM and SPM. The polarization curve, EIS, and SVET results of Q235 steel were obtained by electrochemical testing in HCl and NaCl solutions with different concentrations of inhibitor. The as-prepared IM-CDs suggested an excellent corrosion inhibition effect on steel in both HCl and NaCl solutions, and the inhibition ability enhanced with the increase of inhibitor concentration and immersion time. By calculation from Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the ΔG
ads values of steel in HCl and NaCl environments with inhibitor were −29.23 and −25.45 kJ⋅mol−1, respectively, indicating that the corrosion inhibition of IM-CDs followed physicochemical adsorption mechanism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully appreciate financial support provided by National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 51825505); Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (no. QYZDY-SSW-JSC009); Zhejiang Province Key Technology Project (2015C01006); “One Hundred Talented People” of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Y60707WR04); Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (Y16B040008); K.C. Wong Mgana Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:para:
